paragraphs.body: I have lived in Pittsburgh all my life. I have been coming to school in Oakland for more than 4 years. Apart from 9th grade, I attended virtually in my bedroom as everyone else did during 2020-2021. All my high school years and now my current university years I have spent on 5th avenue. It wasn’t until the week of August 18th that I moved into Nordenberg Hall on 5th Avenue and University Place that I spotted it. Just a quick walk across the street on the opposite corner of the Nordenberg entrance was a storm drain that smelled particularly gross. This isn’t the strangest part of the road thought, because wedged into the corner of the storm drain and the concrete was an old, battered baseball.

The bright red stitching is already starting to come undone, indicating how long it may have been rotting down there. The standard sharp white façade had long been faded. It was more of a mixture of beige, grey and white. Spotted with specks of dirt and whatever other grime finds its way to the storm drain. If I ever dared to touch it, I would assume the sleek, smooth texture would be gone. Replaced by rough edges and a bumpy surface. Judging by how tight the ball is wedged into the road, the ball has lost its circular shape and warped into something else. I imagine that even after all this time, if someone were to pry it out of the ground and decided to play some catch, that the classic whoosh and crunch of throwing the ball into the glove would sound the same. If someone were to lose a bet and be forced to stoop down low and have a taste of the grimy ball, it wouldn’t be pleasant. It would taste of dirt, dust, rotten food, empty wrappers, and whatever other trash finds its way into the storm drain. If someone were to have a taste, they would also get a whiff of the most putrid smell ever. Standing up next to the storm drain, you can smell the rotten waste. Even after you cross the street, the smell follows you, trailing you until you walk at least 15-20 feet away.

annotations.comment: great job with description! I love the way you focus your details on the tattered, smelly, less than pristine aspects of the baseball :)
annotations.context_left: you cross the street, the smell follows you, trailing you until you walk at least 15-20 feet away.

paragraphs.body: I used to play baseball as a kid. Although to be completely honest, I never really enjoyed the sport. I never watched baseball on TV, I rarely went to see the Pirates play, and I preferred to play soccer or football. Despite this, I started playing on a little league team when I was 12 or 13. My best friend always loved to play baseball and had played the sport as soon as he was old enough to hold a bat and through a ball. I joined the Arizona Diamondbacks. It took me some time to adapt, but I eventually found a spot playing in outfield or sometimes even at catcher. We won the little league championship the year I joined. The year after that I quit. This was the start of me trying something else in my life. I was still young, but I wanted to do the things that brought meaning and value to me. Despite winning it all, I never felt a connection to then sport.

annotations.text: used to play baseball as a kid. Although to be completely honest, I never really enjoyed the sport.
annotations.comment: I still think this is such a great transition :) Well done

paragraphs.body: Back during elementary school, my family and I went on a mission trip to Haiti with a church group. While we were there, we took one vacation day apart and went to visit the massive fortress up in the mountainside, The Citadel. Our trip started with us taking a long and winding rocky road up the mountain, on the backs of donkeys. The smell was putrid, and the heat was unbearable. Wearing sunglasses and a hat was almost mandatory, as well as loading up on water. I don’t remember how many times I had to reapply my sunscreen. The sun beating down on us all day, making us sweat and draining our energy. After what seemed like an eternity on that steep narrow road to the top, we made it. Even though we didn’t walk to the top, it felt like we had, judging by how tired we were. What made it all worth it was the immense beauty and wonder of the fortress. Standing at the top, the sun gleaming in the sky revealing the beautiful landscape for miles and miles on end.

annotations.text: Standing at the top, the sun gleaming in the sky revealing the beautiful landscape for miles and miles on end.

annotations.comment: I wonder if you need a connecting idea or two to transition between baseball (which you do not love) and Haiti (which you do)?
annotations.context_right: Back during elementary school, my family and I went on a mission trip to Haiti with

paragraphs.body: My trips to Haiti when I was little were always filled with questions and curiosities. Since Haiti is such a drastically different place from the states in terms of language, culture and everyday life, there is a lot for pre-teen me to adapt to. Arriving at the airport in the capital, Port-Au-Prince, everything seemed to be moving at a million miles per hour. There seemed to be no regard for traffic laws, and every few seconds a vendor is trying to sell you something. We had to very careful for our safety there, and watchful on the streets as it defiantly wasn’t very clean. But the people we met were all so filled with joy and humility, even in some of the worst conditions in the Western Hemisphere, people still seemed to be doing ok.

annotations.text: But the people we met were all so filled with joy and humility, even in some of the worst conditions in the Western Hemisphere, people still seemed to be doing
annotations.comment: I wonder if this idea is at the heart of this essay? Does it need a bit more explanation?

paragraphs.body: One experience I won’t forget from that trip was a ride on a motorcycle. After we had arrived to one of Haitian coordinators, Jewels house, a little way outside the capital. My dad and I were outside talking to one of Jewels sons about this cool red motorcycle he had, and he said he could take me on a little joy ride around the neighborhood. This was a very new and different place for me, so I was very reluctant to go without my dad. My dad, on the other hand, gave the go-ahead, and told me I would be fine. So off I went on the back of the motorcycle through the bustling streets of Port-Au-Prince. I was scared out of my mind, unable to enjoy the trip, and kept asking to go on back. Jewels son was just laughing the whole time and even bought a coke off a street vendor for me. Looking back, my fear was understandable for a young kid, but now, I wish I could have just sat back and enjoyed the ride, because I doubt, I’ll ever experience something like that again.

annotations.text: was scared out of my mind, unable to enjoy the trip, and kept asking to go on back. Jewels son was just laughing the whole time and even bought a coke off a street vendor for me. Looking back, my fear was understandable for a young kid, but now, I wish I could have just sat back and enjoyed the ride, because I doubt, I’ll ever experience something like that again.
annotations.comment: Great moment! This is a wonderful narrative. I love the voice and the details. :)

paragraphs.body: This odd object essay has opened some new thoughts for me and asked some new questions. But it has also answered many of the questions I was asking from the start. The obvious one is what about baseball? Why is there an old baseball sitting in a storm drain? How did it get there? What does it mean? While unfortunately some of these questions will remain a mystery, one I can answer is what does that baseball represent to me? To me, it represents myself, and the human experience. How did I get here, what experience shaped me to be the man I am today. For one, my trip to Haiti had a profound impact on who I am, and how I thought about people, places and things that are new or different to me. I want to reflect those positive and even negative experience that shaped me who I am. My trip to the citadel and enjoying working to conquer something difficult and reap the benefits. My scary motorcycle trip with none of my family challenged my fears and helped me grow to enjoy life’s surprises. Also to chase after things that matter to me, not settle for something that doesn’t apply or appeal to me, like playing baseball. Thinking about the baseball all these months walking by helps me realize what in life is important to me, and what is worth going after.

annotations.text: Thinking about the baseball all these months walking by helps me realize what in life is important to me, and what is worth going after.

annotations.comment: Great revisions and additions! I'm especially taken by the motorcycle paragraph. The voice is excellent and authentic and the details are wonderful. It creates such an interesting foil to the paragraph about how you don't like baseball that much. Your central focusing idea is now clear, though I wonder if the essay would be a bit more cohesive with a few connecting/transitional ideas and sentences here and there. I'm also not seeing a complete definition paragraph. Overall, though, this is a successful essay with a really engaging voice, excellent details, and a lot of range and breadth. It's really fun to read. Well done :)
